concepts.title: Definition: Ricci-flat Kähler manifolds
concepts.blurb: A Calabi–Yau manifold is a compact complex Kähler manifold $X$ of complex dimension $n$ with vanishing first Chern class $c_1(X) = 0$ — equivalently, trivial canonical bundle $K_X \cong \mathcal{O}_X$, equivalently a global holomorphic $n$-form $\Omega$ that vanishes nowhere. Yau's theorem (proving the Calabi conjecture) says each Kähler class then contains a unique Ricci-flat metric: complex algebra forces real geometry.

concepts.title: The Hodge diamond
concepts.blurb: Hodge numbers $h^{p,q} = \dim H^q(X, \Omega^p)$ form a diamond. For a CY $n$-fold, triviality of $K_X$ gives $h^{n,0} = h^{0,0} = 1$, and Kähler / Serre duality forces $h^{p,q} = h^{q,p} = h^{n-p,n-q}$. For a CY 3-fold the only free entries are $h^{1,1}$ and $h^{2,1}$, and the Euler characteristic is $\chi(X) = 2(h^{1,1} - h^{2,1})$.

concepts.title: Examples: elliptic curves, K3, the quintic
concepts.blurb: Dimension 1 CY = elliptic curve (a torus $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$). Dimension 2 CY = K3 surface, e.g. a smooth quartic in $\mathbb{P}^3$, with Hodge diamond $1, 0, 1, 20, 1, 0, 1$. The canonical 3-fold example is the quintic $\{f_5(x_0,\ldots,x_4)=0\} \subset \mathbb{P}^4$, which has $h^{1,1}=1$ and $h^{2,1}=101$. More generally the Batyrev–Borisov construction gives CY hypersurfaces in toric Fano varieties.

concepts.title: Periods and Picard–Fuchs
concepts.blurb: As $X$ varies in a family $X_t$, integrate the holomorphic $n$-form $\Omega_t$ against a basis $\gamma_i$ of $H_n(X,\mathbb{Z})$ to get period integrals $\Pi_i(t) = \int_{\gamma_i} \Omega_t$. These satisfy a linear ODE — the Picard–Fuchs equation — whose monodromy around singular points of moduli encodes the variation of Hodge structure. For the quintic family this is a hypergeometric ${}_4F_3$.

concepts.title: Mirror symmetry: swapping the diamond
concepts.blurb: Mirror symmetry pairs a CY 3-fold $X$ with a mirror $Y$ such that $h^{1,1}(X) = h^{2,1}(Y)$ and $h^{2,1}(X) = h^{1,1}(Y)$ — the Hodge diamond reflects across its diagonal. Physically this is type IIA on $X$ versus type IIB on $Y$. Mathematically it predicts equalities between $A$-model (symplectic, Gromov–Witten) data on $X$ and $B$-model (complex, period) data on $Y$ — the celebrated Candelas–de la Ossa–Green–Parkes count of rational curves on the quintic.

concepts.title: String compactifications, DT, SYZ
concepts.blurb: CY 3-folds are the compact internal directions in superstring compactifications: the $\mathcal{N}=1$ supersymmetry of the 4D effective theory needs Ricci-flat Kähler, hence CY. Donaldson–Thomas invariants count ideal sheaves on $X$ (mirror to Gromov–Witten on $Y$). The Strominger–Yau–Zaslow conjecture realises mirror pairs concretely: every CY 3-fold should admit a special-Lagrangian $T^3$-fibration $X \to B$, with the mirror obtained by dualising the torus fibres.
